My Data Science Portfolio Preface

My Data Science Portfolio Robin Jansen 2026-04-24 Preface Welcome to my Data Science for Biology portfolio, developed as part of the Workflows course at HU University of Applied Sciences Utrecht. This portfolio showcases the skills I have built over the course of my studies — spanning reproducible data analysis, bioinformatics, data management, SQL, and R programming. The work collected here reflects both technical competency and scientific thinking, and is intended to give a clear picture of what I can bring to a research or industry setting. The chapters cover a range of topics: from single-cell RNA sequencing analysis and C. elegans toxicology to database management and reproducible reporting. Where work is still in progress, this is indicated clearly. Robin Jansen Life Sciences — Data Science for Biology HU University of Applied Sciences Utrecht robinjansentilburg@icloud.com github.com/klefkifan55

1 Professional Profile & Future Ambitions 1.1 Curriculum Vitae 1.2 Vision for the Future

1 Professional Profile & Future Ambitions 1.1 Curriculum Vitae 1.1.1 Robin Jansen Life Sciences & Data Science for Biology student HU University of Applied Sciences Utrecht robinjansentilburg@icloud.com | github.com/klefkifan55 1.1.2 Education Bachelor of Life Sciences — Data Science for Biology track HU University of Applied Sciences Utrecht | 2022 – Present (Year 3) 1.1.3 Technical Skills Category Skills Programming R (proficient), Bash (proficient), SQL (in progress) R Packages tidyverse, ggplot2, bookdown, Rmarkdown, rollama, querychat, Seurat Bioinformatics Nanopore sequencing analysis, taxonomic classification (Kraken2/Bracken), Krona visualization Data Management Git, GitHub, reproducible workflows, Guerilla Analytics principles Genomics VCF format, scRNA-seq analysis (Seurat pipeline), ALS genomics data Other RStudio, DBeaver, PostgreSQL, bookdown portfolio hosting (GitHub Pages) 1.1.4 Projects & Experience Projecticum — Project ALS Database | 2026 Working within the research group of Kevin Kenna (UMC Utrecht) on Project MinE, an international ALS genomics consortium. Developing a natural language processing workflow using querychat and rollama (Ollama/LLaMA3) to make a complex VCF-format SQL database accessible to non-technical scientists. This involves integrating a local large language model to translate natural language queries into PostgreSQL queries. This project is still currently ongoing. Periodontitis Metagenomics Project | 2025 Performed taxonomic classification of Nanopore sequencing data from periodontitis samples using Bash-based pipelines. Applied Kraken2 and Bracken for species-level identification and generated interactive Krona plots to visualize microbial community composition. 1.1.5 Core Competencies Reproducible and documented data analysis workflows Translating complex biological questions into computational pipelines Working in collaborative GitHub-based projects Independent learning and applying new bioinformatics tools 1.2 Vision for the Future 1.2.1 Where do I want to be in 1–2 years? My ambitions lie in the field of genomics and bioinformatics, though I am still in the process of finding out exactly which direction suits me best. Both wet lab research and computational/in silico work genuinely interest me, and I see value in developing skills across both worlds. What I do know is that I want to work in an environment where genomic data plays a central role — whether that is in a research group, a clinical setting, or a biotech company. Areas like transcriptomics, rare disease genomics, and precision medicine appeal to me. Over the next one to two years, my goal is to secure an internship that exposes me to professional research workflows and helps me find out where my strengths and interests are best applied. 1.2.2 Where do I stand now? I have a solid foundation in R-based data analysis and reproducible workflows. My experience with the ALS project will give me hands-on exposure to SQL databases, VCF-format genomic data, and integrating LLMs into bioinformatics tools. My periodontitis project built real competency in Bash-based sequencing pipelines and taxonomic analysis. That said, I am still developing in several areas — particularly in Python (widely used in bioinformatics), scRNA-seq analysis, and large-scale pipeline tools such as Snakemake or Nextflow. With Snakemake being a python-based workflow manager. 1.2.3 Skills I still want to develop scRNA-seq analysis — deepening expertise in Seurat, cell type annotation, trajectory analysis (this is also my free assignment) Python — essential for many bioinformatics tools; I intend to start building this after the main course of R finishes. Workflow managers — Snakemake or Nextflow for scalable, reproducible pipelines Scientific writing — preparing results in publication-ready format The free assignment described in the next chapter is a direct step toward one of these goals: building real, applied competency in single-cell RNA sequencing analysis using Seurat.
learning-single-cell-rna-sequencing-analysis-with-seurat.html

2 Learning Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Analysis with Seurat 2.1 Background & Motivation 2.2 Planning 2.3 The scRNA-seq Pipeline with Seurat 2.4 Key Tools & Resources 2.5 Using the pipeline myself 2.6 Results & Reflection

2 Learning Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Analysis with Seurat 2.1 Background & Motivation Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has fundamentally changed how we study gene expression. Rather than averaging signals across millions of cells in a bulk sample, scRNA-seq allows us to profile gene expression in each individual cell — revealing cell-type composition, rare populations, developmental trajectories, and disease-specific transcriptional states. This technology is increasingly central to research in oncology (tumor heterogeneity), immunology, neuroscience, and rare disease genomics — all areas that overlap with my future ambitions in bioinformatics. In the pharmaceutical industry specifically, scRNA-seq is used for drug mechanism studies, biomarker discovery, and understanding how different cell populations respond to treatment. Given that my Projecticum project deals with ALS genomics, where understanding cell-type-specific gene expression in motor neurons is highly relevant, learning scRNA-seq analysis is a natural and motivated next step. 2.1.1 Connection to the future plan As described in the previous chapter, I aim to work in bioinformatics or genomics research. scRNA-seq competency is listed as a desired skill in virtually every junior bioinformatics role in the field today. This assignment gives me a structured opportunity to build that skill from the ground up — starting with a tutorial dataset and ending with an independent analysis on a novel dataset. 2.2 Planning The free assignment is budgeted at 32 hours. The table below shows how I allocated this time: Phase Activity Hours 1 Seurat tutorial on PBMC 3k dataset 8 hrs 2 Apply pipeline to new dataset of choice 12 hrs 3 Write-up, code annotation, figure generation 8 hrs 4 Buffer — debugging, troubleshooting 4 hrs Total 32 hrs How will I demonstrate the skill? The output will be a fully annotated R script and a set of UMAP figures showing distinct cell populations in a self-chosen dataset. The chosen dataset will be a publicly available scRNA-seq dataset. I will try to select one relevant to ALS or neurodegeneration to connect this skill to my broader project context. 2.3 The scRNA-seq Pipeline with Seurat The standard Seurat pipeline takes a count matrix (genes × cells) as input — this is the output of upstream processing tools like Cell Ranger and represents how many reads of each gene were detected per cell. The steps below describe the full analysis workflow. Tutorial can be found at https://satijalab.org/seurat/articles/pbmc3k_tutorial 2.3.1 Step 1 — Loading Data library(Seurat) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(patchwork) # Load the PBMC 3k dataset (available via Seurat tutorial) pbmc.data <- Read10X(data.dir = "~/dsfb2_workflows_portfolio/processed_data/filtered_gene_bc_matrices/hg19/") pbmc <- CreateSeuratObject(counts = pbmc.data, project = "pbmc3k", min.cells = 3, min.features = 200) 2.3.2 Step 2 — Quality Control & Filtering Low-quality cells are identified and removed based on three metrics: nFeature_RNA — number of unique genes detected per cell (too low = dead cell; too high = doublet) nCount_RNA — total number of RNA molecules per cell percent.mt — percentage of reads mapping to mitochondrial genes (high = dying cell) pbmc[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(pbmc, pattern = "^MT-") VlnPlot(pbmc, features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "percent.mt"), ncol = 3) pbmc <- subset(pbmc, subset = nFeature_RNA > 200 & nFeature_RNA < 2500 & percent.mt < 5) 2.3.3 Step 3 — Normalization Each cell’s gene counts are normalized to account for differences in sequencing depth, then log-transformed. pbmc <- NormalizeData(pbmc, normalization.method = "LogNormalize", scale.factor = 10000) 2.3.4 Step 4 — Feature Selection Highly variable genes (HVGs) — genes that show meaningful variation across cells — are selected for downstream analysis. This reduces noise and computational load. pbmc <- FindVariableFeatures(pbmc, selection.method = "vst", nfeatures = 2000) 2.3.5 Step 5 — Scaling Data is scaled so that each gene has a mean of 0 and variance of 1 across cells, which is required for PCA. all.genes <- rownames(pbmc) pbmc <- ScaleData(pbmc, features = all.genes) 2.3.6 Step 6 — PCA Principal component analysis reduces the high-dimensional gene expression space to a manageable number of principal components (PCs). pbmc <- RunPCA(pbmc, features = VariableFeatures(object = pbmc)) ElbowPlot(pbmc) # used to decide how many PCs to use 2.3.7 Step 7 — Clustering A nearest-neighbor graph is constructed in PC space, then the Louvain algorithm identifies clusters of transcriptionally similar cells. pbmc <- FindNeighbors(pbmc, dims = 1:10) pbmc <- FindClusters(pbmc, resolution = 0.5) 2.3.8 Step 8 — UMAP Visualization UMAP projects the high-dimensional data into 2D for visual exploration of clusters. pbmc <- RunUMAP(pbmc, dims = 1:10) DimPlot(pbmc, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE) 2.3.9 Step 9 — Marker Gene Identification Differentially expressed genes that define each cluster are identified. pbmc.markers <- FindAllMarkers(pbmc, only.pos = TRUE, min.pct = 0.25, logfc.threshold = 0.25) 2.3.10 Step 10 — Cell Type Annotation Marker genes are matched to known cell type signatures. For example, in PBMCs: Cluster markers Cell type IL7R, CCR7 Naive CD4+ T cells CD14, LYZ CD14+ Monocytes MS4A1 B cells CD8A CD8+ T cells GNLY, NKG7 NK cells new.cluster.ids <- c("Naive CD4 T", "CD14+ Mono", "Memory CD4 T", "B", "CD8 T", "FCGR3A+ Mono", "NK", "DC", "Platelet") names(new.cluster.ids) <- levels(pbmc) pbmc <- RenameIdents(pbmc, new.cluster.ids) DimPlot(pbmc, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE, pt.size = 0.5) 2.4 Key Tools & Resources Seurat — main R package for scRNA-seq analysis (satijalab.org/seurat) ggplot2 — visualization DoubletFinder — optional doublet detection SingleR — automated cell type annotation using reference datasets OSCA Book — Orchestrating Single-Cell Analysis (bioconductor.org/books/release/OSCA) Good starting datasets: PBMC 3k — classic beginner dataset, available directly via the Seurat tutorial CZ CELLxGENE — large curated collection of scRNA-seq datasets, recommended by professors — cellxgene.cziscience.com 10x Genomics public datasets — genomics.10xgenomics.com GEO (Gene Expression Omnibus) — search scRNA-seq + disease of interest 2.5 Using the pipeline myself 2.6 Results & Reflection

3 Data Management 3.1 The Guerilla Analytics Principles 3.2 3a. RNA-seq Data Management (DAUR2) 3.3 3b. Portfolio Repository

3 Data Management Good data management is foundational to reproducible science. In this chapter, the Guerilla Analytics principles for data management are applied to two contexts: the RNA-seq formatieve opdracht from the DAUR2 module, and my own portfolio repository. 3.1 The Guerilla Analytics Principles Guerilla Analytics is a pragmatic framework for managing analytical data in complex, real-world environments. The core rules most relevant to bioinformatics workflows are: Space is cheap, confusion is expensive — keep raw data raw and never overwrite it Use a single, versioned directory structure — everything has a defined place Separate raw data from derived data — processed files live in separate folders Every dataset should have a README — describe what the data is, where it came from, and how to use it Automate where possible — use scripts rather than manual steps to ensure reproducibility 3.2 3a. RNA-seq Data Management (DAUR2) The RNA-seq formatieve opdracht from the DAUR2 module involves multiple types of data files generated at different stages of the pipeline: raw reads (FASTQ), aligned reads (BAM), and count files. The Guerilla Analytics structure below organizes these files in a clear, reproducible way. 3.2.1 Directory structure library(fs) dir_tree("../references/daur2_rnaseq/") ## ../references/daur2_rnaseq/ ## ├── analysis ## │ └── fastqc_HTML ## │ ├── example_file1.html ## │ ├── example_file2.html ## │ └── example_file3.html ## ├── processed_data ## ├── raw_data ## │ ├── bam ## │ └── fastq ## ├── README ## ├── references ## └── scripts ## ├── bashtest.sh ## ├── fastqc_mv.sh ## ├── fixed2c.sh ## ├── LEBRON.html ## ├── LEBRON.Rmd ## ├── rtest.R ## ├── rtest2.R ## ├── test.html ## └── test.Rmd ### README content # DAUR2 RNA-seq Formatieve Opdracht ## Project description This directory contains data and analysis scripts for the RNA-seq formatieve opdracht from the DAUR2 module (DSFB1 cursus) ## Author Robin Jansen | robinjansentilburg@icloud.com | github.com/klefkifan55 ## Data origin Raw FASTQ files are stored in raw_data/fastq/. BAM files (aligned reads) are stored in raw_data/bam/. FastQC quality control reports are stored in analysis/fastqc_HTML/. ## Directory structure ├── analysis/ │ └── fastqc_HTML/ → FastQC quality control HTML reports ├── processed_data/ → cleaned/processed output files ├── raw_data/ │ ├── fastq/ → raw sequencing reads (do not edit) │ └── bam/ → aligned reads produced by pipeline ├── references/ → reference genome and annotation files └── scripts/ → analysis scripts (bash and R) ## How to reproduce Run bash scripts in order, then R scripts for downstream analysis. All paths are relative to the project root. 3.3 3b. Portfolio Repository The portfolio repository itself is also organized according to Guerilla Analytics principles, ensuring that the bookdown source files, data, and output are clearly separated and documented. 3.3.1 Directory structure library(fs) dir_tree("../") ## The key elements are: raw_data/ — original, unmodified input data that is never edited processed_data/ — cleaned data files and reference datasets used in analyses analysis/ — exploratory analyses and scratch work references/ — literature and reference materials scripts/ — all bookdown .Rmd source files and analysis scripts, including the rendered _book/ output 3.3.2 README content # dsfb2_workflows_portfolio ## Description Portfolio for the Workflows course (Data Science for Biology 2) at HU University of Applied Sciences Utrecht. This portfolio demonstrates skills in reproducible data analysis, data management, SQL, bioinformatics, and R programming. The portfolio is built with bookdown and hosted via GitHub Pages at: https://klefkifan55.github.io ## Author Robin Jansen | robinjansentilburg@icloud.com | github.com/klefkifan55 ## Directory structure dsfb2_workflows_portfolio/ ├── raw_data/ → original, unmodified input data (do not edit) ├── processed_data/ → cleaned/processed data files and reference datasets │ ├── CE.LIQ.FLOW.062_Tidydata.xlsx → C. elegans toxicity data (ch. 4) │ └── filtered_gene_bc_matrices/ → PBMC 3k scRNA-seq data (ch. 2) ├── analysis/ → exploratory analyses and scratch work ├── references/ → literature and reference materials ├── scripts/ → bookdown source files and analysis scripts │ ├── index.Rmd → portfolio homepage │ ├── 01-cv-future.Rmd → CV and future ambitions │ ├── 02-free-assignment.Rmd → scRNA-seq analysis with Seurat │ ├── 03-data-management.Rmd → data management (Guerilla Analytics) │ ├── 04-reproducible.Rmd → reproducible science │ ├── 05-sql.Rmd → SQL databases │ ├── references.bib → BibTeX references │ ├── _bookdown.yml → bookdown configuration │ ├── _output.yml → output format configuration │ └── _book/ → rendered HTML output (do not edit manually) ## How to build Set working directory to scripts/ and run in the R console: bookdown::render_book('.') The rendered site is output to scripts/_book/. Copy the contents of _book/ to the klefkifan55.github.io repository and push to update the live site. ## Portfolio contents (so far) 1. CV and future ambitions 2. Free assignment — scRNA-seq analysis with Seurat 3. Data management 4. Reproducible science ## How to reproduce All analysis scripts are in scripts/ and are numbered in order of execution. Data files are in processed_data/. Paths in the .Rmd files are relative to the scripts/ directory.
reproducible-science.html
4 Reproducible Science 4.1 4a. Reproducing a C. elegans Toxicity Analysis 4.2 4b. Evaluating Reproducibility of a Published Article

4 Reproducible Science Reproducibility is one of the cornerstones of good science. In this chapter, I first reproduce a C. elegans dose-response experiment from a colleague’s data, and then evaluate the reproducibility of a published scientific article with shared R code. 4.1 4a. Reproducing a C. elegans Toxicity Analysis 4.1.1 Background The data for this analysis comes from the HU lectoraat Innovative Testing in Life Sciences & Chemistry. C. elegans (roundworms) were exposed to varying concentrations of different chemicals, and the number of offspring was recorded as a measure of toxicity. This type of data is commonly analysed using dose-response modelling to determine the IC50 — the concentration at which 50% inhibition of reproduction is observed. The variables of interest are: RawData — number of offspring counted compName — name of the chemical compound compConcentration — concentration used expType — whether the observation is experimental, a negative control, or a vehicle control 4.1.2 Step 1 — Loading the data library(readxl) library(tidyverse) ce_data <- read_xlsx("../processed_data/CE.LIQ.FLOW.062_Tidydata.xlsx") 4.1.3 Step 2 — Inspecting the data ## # compUnit <chr>, compDelivery <chr>, compVehicle <chr>, elegansStrain <chr>, elegansInput <dbl>, ## # bacterialStrain <chr>, bacterialTreatment <chr>, bacterialOD600 <dbl>, bacterialConcX <dbl>, bacterialVolume <dbl>, ## # bacterialVolUnit <chr>, incubationVial <chr>, incubationVolume <dbl>, incubationUnit <chr>, incubationMethod <chr>, ## # incubationRPM <dbl>, bubble <lgl>, incubateTemperature <dbl> Before proceeding, we check whether the data types are correct. RawData should be numeric, compName should be a character or factor, and compConcentration should be numeric. It was read as a character column, which is corrected below. Note that control rows have no meaningful concentration value and will produce NA after conversion — this is expected and handled in the plotting steps. 4.1.4 Step 3 — Visualising the raw data Control rows with a concentration of 0 or NA are excluded from the plot, as log10(0) is undefined. These rows represent negative and vehicle controls which do not have a meaningful concentration on a log scale. ce_data %>% filter(!is.na(compConcentration) & compConcentration > 0) %>% ggplot(aes(x = compConcentration, y = RawData, colour = compName, shape = expType)) + geom_point(position = position_jitter(width = 0.05, height = 0), alpha = 0.7, size = 2) + scale_x_log10() + labs( title = "C. elegans offspring count by compound and concentration", x = "Concentration (log10 scale)", y = "Number of offspring (RawData)", colour = "Compound", shape = "Experiment type" ) + theme_minimal() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) Figure 4.1: Scatter plot of C. elegans offspring counts by compound and concentration. The x-axis uses a log10 scale. Different compounds are shown in different colours; shape indicates experiment type. Control observations with no defined concentration are excluded. 4.1.5 Step 4 — Normalisation to the negative control To make results comparable across experiments and plates, the data is normalised to the negative control (controlNegative). The mean of the negative control observations is set to 1, and all other values are expressed as a fraction of this mean. Why normalise? Different experimental runs may yield different absolute offspring counts due to variation in worm health, temperature, or other environmental factors. Normalising to the negative control removes this batch effect and expresses all values as relative to what a healthy, unexposed worm produces under the same conditions. What are the controls? Negative control (controlNegative) — worms exposed to the solvent only (no compound). This represents 100% reproduction. All other values are expressed relative to this. Vehicle control — similar to the negative control, used when the compound is dissolved in something other than water, to check that the vehicle itself has no effect. Positive control — worms exposed to a known toxic compound at a known concentration. This confirms the assay is working as expected; a strong inhibition of reproduction should be observed. ce_data %>% filter(!is.na(compConcentration) & compConcentration > 0) %>% ggplot(aes(x = compConcentration, y = RawData_norm, colour = compName, shape = expType)) + geom_point(position = position_jitter(width = 0.05, height = 0), alpha = 0.7, size = 2) + scale_x_log10() + geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") + labs( title = "Normalised C. elegans offspring count", x = "Concentration (log10 scale)", y = "Normalised offspring count (relative to negative control)", colour = "Compound", shape = "Experiment type" ) + theme_minimal() + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) Figure 4.2: Normalised offspring counts. Values are expressed as a fraction of the mean negative control (= 1). Values below 1 indicate inhibition of reproduction. Control observations with no defined concentration are excluded. 4.1.6 Plan for follow-up: Dose-Response Analysis A natural next step for this data is fitting a log-logistic dose-response model using the drc package in R. The goal is to estimate the IC50 — the concentration at which 50% inhibition of reproduction is observed — for each compound. The steps would be: Install and load the drc package Subset the data to one compound at a time (or fit models per compound using group_by + nest) Fit a four-parameter log-logistic model using drm(RawData_norm ~ compConcentration, fct = LL.4(), data = ...) Inspect model fit with summary() and plot() Extract IC50 values using ED(model, 50, interval = "delta") Repeat for all compounds and compile results into a summary table This approach is described in detail in Ritz et al. (2015), PLOS ONE — a key reference for dose-response modelling in R. 4.2 4b. Evaluating Reproducibility of a Published Article Note: This section is still in progress and will be completed at a later stage. 4.2.1 The article (To be completed — a PLOS ONE article with shared R code will be selected using the search strategy described in the assignment.) 4.2.2 Reproducibility assessment Transparency criterion Definition Assessment Study Purpose Clear statement of research objective in introduction — Data Availability Statement Explicit statement about data access — Data Location Where data can be accessed — Study Location Country/region of data origin stated in methods — Author Review Contact information professionalism — Ethics Statement Statement on ethical considerations — Funding Statement Statement on funding — Code Availability R code shared and accessible — 4.2.3 Code readability (To be completed) 4.2.4 Reproduction attempt (To be completed)
working-with-sql-databases.html
5 Working with SQL Databases

5 Working with SQL Databases Structured Query Language (SQL) is an essential tool for any data scientist working with large or complex datasets. In this chapter, I demonstrate how to load, clean, and integrate three biological datasets — dengue fever, influenza, and the Gapminder demographic dataset — into a PostgreSQL database, and use SQL queries to explore and join the data.
